coredata.dc:title: Facilitate nitrogen fixation photoreaction over decorated composited dots on the (001) facet exposed anatase titanium dioxide nanosheets
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Huaiwei
Bao, Liang
Zhou, Qingwei
Song, Ge
coredata.dc:description: Anatase TiO2-based materials have received increasing attentions in the photocatalytic ammonia synthesis field, but the lower nitrogen absorption abilities and photoinduced charge carrier separation efficiencies restrict their further development. Herein, anatase titanium dioxide nanosheets modified with carbon dots/cobalt composite on the exposed (001) facets are designed through a simple strategy. The co-decorated photocatalyst presents a strong photo adsorption in the visible-light region, and strong chemisorption ability is received. The photocatalytic ammonia synthesis rates are approximately 162 μmol·gcal −1·h−1 without any additives, increasing approximately 4 times compared to the original TiO2. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) at 420 nm reaches up to 0.6 %. Compared to the commercial nano-TiO2 and as-prepared mesoscopic TiO2, the modified (001) exposed TiO2 sample also has the best photocatalytic activity. Doping suitable modification structures on the appropriate exposed crystal facet of anatase TiO2 can further improve the nitrogen absorption and charge separation process. This study provides a new insight for the fabrication strategy of photo-catalytic ammonia synthesis materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
N2 fixation
Exposed facet
Adsorption active sites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns (a); Partial enlarged XRD curves (b, c); Raman spectra (d, e) of the various modified samples.
XPS analyses of the modified samples (a) Ti 2p; (b)Co 2p; (c) O 1 s.
Structural characterization of the samples. (a,b) TEM images and (c) HRTEM image of Ti-e sample; (d, e) TEM images, (f) TEM-EDS mapping and (g, h) HRTEM images of TiCoC-e sample.

(a) NH3 synthesis rates under light illumination; (b,) NH3 produced yields catalyzed by 1 g TiCoC-e catalyst with light illumination under N2 or Ar; (c) NH3 yields catalyzed by 1 g TiCoC-e catalyst with/without lighting at 0.5 h interval under N2 gas; (d) N2-tpd analyses of the as-prepared photocatalysts.
(a) UV–vis DRS spectra, (b) The corresponding Tauc curves of (a), (c) VB-XPS curves and (d) Energy band positions of Ti-e, TiC-e, TiCo-e and TiCoC-e samples.

(a) NH3 production rates under lighting of the modified samples; (b) Calculated AQEs over the TiCoC-e and TiCoC-b under light illumination with wavelength of 365 nm, 420 nm, 520 nm and 625 nm; (c) Steady-state PL spectra (the inset image is the TRPL plots); (d) transient photocurrent responses of the various comparative samples catalysts; (e) Energy band structures and improved N2 fixation process of the modified samples.

head.ce:title: Facilitate nitrogen fixation photoreaction over decorated composited dots on the (001) facet exposed anatase titanium dioxide nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analysis and Testing Center, School of medicine, Shanghai university, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Analysis and Testing Center, School of medicine, Shanghai university

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analysis and Testing Center, School of medicine, Shanghai university, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Anatase TiO2-based materials have received increasing attentions in the photocatalytic ammonia synthesis field, but the lower nitrogen absorption abilities and photoinduced charge carrier separation efficiencies restrict their further development. Herein, anatase titanium dioxide nanosheets modified with carbon dots/cobalt composite on the exposed (001) facets are designed through a simple strategy. The co-decorated photocatalyst presents a strong photo adsorption in the visible-light region, and strong chemisorption ability is received. The photocatalytic ammonia synthesis rates are approximately 162 μmol·gcal −1·h−1 without any additives, increasing approximately 4 times compared to the original TiO2. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) at 420 nm reaches up to 0.6 %. Compared to the commercial nano-TiO2 and as-prepared mesoscopic TiO2, the modified (001) exposed TiO2 sample also has the best photocatalytic activity. Doping suitable modification structures on the appropriate exposed crystal facet of anatase TiO2 can further improve the nitrogen absorption and charge separation process. This study provides a new insight for the fabrication strategy of photo-catalytic ammonia synthesis materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia as the important industrial raw material has been successfully applied to many fields. Nitrogen photo-fixation process (2N2 + 6H2O = 4NH3 + 3O2) under the ambient conditions is an efficient ammonia production strategy to replace the traditional Haber-Bosch method [1,2], and how to break the stable NN triple bonds is an enormous challenge [3–5]. However, the low visible light utilization and weak N2 absorption activity for the existing photocatalytic materials are necessary to solve key technical problems. In this regard, it urgently needs to fabricate more reaction and absorption active sites on the catalysts to further meet the current requirements [6–9].

Transition metal-based oxides are considered as one type of promising semiconductor materials which can be applied to photocatalytic ammonia synthesis process [10–14]. Among them, TiO2 based materials have been attracted more attention in recent years due to their unique electronic structures and photoreaction characteristics. However, there are still some common drawbacks such as lower absorption active sites and rapid photoinduced electron/hole recombination restricting the further application during the nitrogen fixation process [15–21]. Fabrication of surface defects is the effective method to adjust the energy band structure for improved catalytic active sites and reactant molecules adsorption. The enhanced nitrogen photo-fixation performance has been obtained through introducing the oxygen defects by doping the foreign elements on TiO2. But ammonia production efficiency is still inefficient due to the low N2 absorption capacity [22–24].

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increased concerns due to the excellent N2 molecules absorption ability, higher conductivity and lower cost [25–27]. Therefore, the CDS become a suitable decorated mediator to fabricate the new photocatalysts. Li et al. reported a floatable photocatalyst of TiO2/Bi/ Carbon cloth, and the enhanced photocatalytic ammonia production rates were received through relay pathway [28]. Single yttrium sites anchored on carbon-coated TiO2 can dramatically improve the N2 absorption activity [29]. Furthermore, amounts of hydroxyl radical can be generated on (001)-facet-exposed anatase TiO2 nanosheets [30–32], and these types of radicals are always regarded as the key active species in the photoreaction. It is thus reasonable to expect that anatase TiO2 with (001) exposed facet can be an efficient photocatalyst.

Based on the discussion, in this work, CDs/Co composite modified anatase TiO2 nanosheets with exposed (001) facet are successfully constructed through a simple strategy firstly, which can obtain the excellent photo response in the visible light region. Impressively, the optimized sample exhibits a robust N2 photo-fixation ability, increasing several times compared to the original. The improved photocatalytic performances can give the credit to the improved N2 molecules chemisorption activity and photo-induced charge separation efficiency. This strategy provides a new development idea for the efficient design of ammonia synthesis photocatalysts to further utilize the sunlight energy more efficiently.

Tetrabutyl titanate (C16H36O4Ti, > 99 %), titanium isopropoxide (C12H28O4Ti, > 95 %), hydrofluoric acid (HF, ≥ 40 %), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O, > 98 %), acetone (C3H6O, > 99.5 %), commercial nano-TiO2 (anatase TiO2, < 60 nm, > 99.8 %, named as Ti-b), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, > 97 %), glucose (C6H12O6, > 98 %), potassium sodium tartrate tetrahydrate (KNaC4H12O10·4H2O, 1 mol/L) and Nessler’s reagent (K2HgI4) were all purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The purity Argon (Ar) and nitrogen (N2) were obtained from Minxing Huagong Technology Co., Ltd. (Hangzhou, China). All chemicals are analytical grade and used without further purification.

First, the raw exposed (001) facet TiO2 (Ti-e) and mesoscopic TiO2 (Ti-m) samples are synthesized using the previous reported hydrothermal synthesis method [30,33,34]. The commercial TiO2 (Ti-b) with the uniform nanostructure exhibits a high crystallinity, which is purchased from a professional reagent company. The specific synthetic methods are shown in supplementary document. Co-decorated TiO2 samples are synthesized via a solvent impregnated process. Typically, 100 mg of as-prepared Ti-e precursors and 20 mg of cobalt chloride hexahydrate are put into 1 mL of distilled water to obtain the slurry under continuous stirring. The as-prepared muds are dried at 333 K for 24 h, and then heat-treated at 673 K for 4 h in a tube furnace with a rate of 10 K/min. The obtained catalyst is named as TiCo-e. Finally, 100 mg of TiCo-e sample and 10 mg of glucose are dissolving in 30 mL of distilled water in an autoclave, and then heated at 443 K for 12 h to obtain the photocatalyst for TiCoC-e. As the contrastive samples, the TiCoC-b and TiCoC-m are prepared by the same process except the replacement of Ti-e with Ti-b and Ti-m, respectively.

Photocatalytic nitrogen fixations of the various catalysts are evoluted through the ammonia production yields under the light irradiation by a 300 W Xe lamp. Before the photocatalytic process, 20 mg of samples and 250 mL of distilled water are added into a self-regulation quartz reaction vessel (Fig.S7). The experimental temperature maintains at the room temperature via circulating water cooling system after the light source. The inlet gas pressure is kept between (0.1 ∼ 0.15) MPa. The reactor is purged with high pure N2 for 30 min in dark. Subsequently, under continues magnetic stirring, the vessel is illuminated used Xe lamp for 1 h. The produced ammonia is determined via the Nessler's reagent (Fig.S1) and ion chromatography methods. Specifically, 20 mL clarified solution is mixed with 1 mL potassium sodium tartrate solution (500 g·L−1) and 1.5 mL Nessler’s reagent, respectively. After standing 10 min, 5 mL of the above solution is used for a UV–vis absorbance measurement at 420 nm. The ammonia yield can be obtained through the absorbance-concentration standard curve. The specific contents are shown in supplied materials.

The AQE measured process is essentially as good as the photocatalytic N2 fixation experiments except that under different monochromatic light wavelengths (365 nm, 420 nm, 520 nm and 620 nm) irradiation using the filters with a bandwidth of ± 5 nm and 50 mg photocatalyst in 100 mL deionized water. The light intensities are 0.9 mW/cm2, 5.5 mW/cm2, 9.2 mW/cm2 and 11.7 mW/cm2 under the wavelength of 365 nm, 420 nm, 520 nm and 620 nm, respectively. The value of light intensity is measured by a photo radiometer. The catalytic reactions for determining AQE are carried out in a quartz vessel with the irradiation area of 1 cm2. The AQE is calculated as following equation:
AQE =
Number of generated N
H
3
* 3
Number of incident photons
=
n
NH 3
×
N
A
× 3
W × A × t
h × v

Where n
NH3 is the molar number of generated NH3; N
A and h are the he Avogadro’s constant and Planck constant; W, ν, A and t are the irradiation light intensity, frequency, irradiation area and time, respectively.

The phases and compositions of the as-prepared photo-catalysts are detected through the XRD measurement in Fig. 1 and Fig.S3. The diffraction peaks of the three types of TiO2-based samples are identical to the standard anatase phase (JCPDS #21–1272). As compared with Ti-m and Ti-b, the Ti-e sample has a decreased weaker diffraction peak of (004), which indicates a reduced thickness in the (001) direction in TiO2 nanosheets. Some similar results were also obtained in the previous study [30]. Comparing the partial amplified spectra, the Co3O4 phase exists in the TiCo-e sample rather than in the Ti-e, and the diffraction peaks at approximately 31.3°, 37.0° and 38.6° correlate to the (220), (311) and (222) planes of the cubic Co3O4 (JCPDS #42–1467), respectively. Nevertheless, the Co3O4 phase is transformed into the Co(OH)2 in the TiCoC-e after the reduction process with glucose, and the peaks at approximately 37.7° and 38.4° correspond to the (101) and (002) planes (JCPDS #45–0031), respectively. The accurate cobalt and carbon contents with 2.42 wt% and 0.94 wt% are determined by inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) and elements analyzer methods, respectively.

The surface conditions are further verified via the Raman spectra in Fig. 1(c, d). Pristine Ti-e sample exhibits vibrational peaks at approximately 145, 395, 512, and 634 cm−1, which belong to Eg, B1g, A1g, and Eg vibration modes of Ti-O bonds in anatase TiO2 [35,36]. As the modification process, Raman peaks display broadening and shift of the Eg mode to higher wavenumbers, which is mostly due to the phonon confinement effect caused by localized defects [37]. Two broader peaks at 1360 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 are given the credits to the carbon-based G and D characteristic bands, respectively [38]. Compared to the Ti-e and TiCo-e, there are obvious carbon peaks in the TiC-e and TiCoC-e samples, which illustrated that extra carbon dots (CDs) with the low crystallinity are generated.

The XPS curves of the photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 2 and Fig.S4. Specifically, the binding energies of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 are reduced 458.9 eV and 464.7 eV of Ti-e to 458.6 and 464.4 of TiCoC-e in Fig. 2a, respectively. Thus, the electron density enhancement reflects the formation of the isolated Ti3+ and oxygen defects in the photocatalyst, matching the Raman results [28,39]. These defects can usually act as the reaction active sites during the photo catalytic process. To further clarify the cobalt element in the materials, XPS spectra for Co 2p are also analyzed in Fig. 2b. Comparing to TiCo-e, the peaks of Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 all shift to the high binding energy direction for the TiCoC-e sample. The peak at 780.5 eV assigned to Co 2p3/2 in the TiCo-e sample corresponded to the Co element for the Co3O4, and the peak at about 781 eV in the TiCoC-e is marked as the Co2+ for the Co(OH)2 [40,41]. These results agree with the XRD analyses. In Fig. 2c, the peaks at 531.5 eV and 530 eV are corresponding to the surface oxygen defects (Ov) and lattice oxygen (Ol) [42,43]. More oxygen vacancies are formed on the defect sites after modification process.

From the microstructure of the original TiO2 nanosheets ( Fig. 3a, b), the unique anatase TiO2-based nanosheets with a rectangular shape with the length of approximately 50–80 nm and thickness of approximately 8.9 nm are present, which are taken along the perpendicular direction. The HRTEM picture (Fig. 3c) displays that two lattice fringes with the spacing of 0.19 nm corresponding to the anatase TiO2 (200) lattice plane and 0.35 nm corresponding to the anatase TiO2 (101) lattice plane are observed, respectively, and the vector angle of these two facets is 90°. Therefore, the top and bottom facets of the nanosheets in Fig. 3a are (001), which are perpendicular to the diffraction plane. There are higher exposed (001) facets in the sample. The ratio of the peak intensity of (101) to (200) in the XRD patterns (Fig. S1) is 1.6 for the Ti-e sample, which is also in line with the law of the exposure of (001) facets according to the previous study [44]. As the comparative samples, the ratio is 2.6 or 3.2 for the commercial nano-TiO2 (Ti-b) or mesoscopic TiO2 (Ti-m), respectively. According to the size of the Ti-e nanosheets, the percentage of exposed (001) facets of 86.7 % can be evaluated approximately (Ti-e nanosheets with an average length and width of 67 nm and a thickness of 8.9 nm). The modified TiCoC-e sample is also characterized through HRTEM analyses. As shown in Fig. 3(d,e), the similar rectangular shape and structural character are kept after the hydrothermal reaction process. Comparing to the original, small clusters structures can be observed on the modified sample surface (circled in red in Fig. 3e). Every element of Ti, O and Co is also distributed uniformly in Fig. 2f. Carbon-based nanoparticles with the diameter of 3 nm ∼ 5 nm are loaded on the photocatalyst surface in the Fig. 3g, and the lattice spacing is 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) interplanar. Furthermore, the embedded Co(OH)2 nanosheets with lattice spacing of 0.24 nm assigned to the (101) interplanar can be also detected in the sample (Fig. 3g).

The photocatalytic N2 fixation rates of the as-prepared materials are concluded in Fig. 4a. The pristine exposed anatase TiO2, carbon and cobalt-decorated TiO2 samples (Ti-e, TiC-e and TiCo-e) both have the low photocatalytic activities. The ammonia yield rates can achieve up to 162 and 83 μmol·gcal −1·h−1 under full-spectrum and visible light illumination without any additives for the composited dots decorated sample (TiCoC-e), increasing approximately 4 times compared to the original sample. This is one of the best reported production rates of the TiO2-based photocatalytic ammonia synthesis materials (Table.S1). The ammonia produced yield is also confirmed through the ion chromatograph (IC) measurement (Fig.S5). Moreover, NH3 amounts catalyzed by 1 g TiCoC-e under N2 or Ar flow and alternating lighting at 0.5 h interval are shown in Fig. 4(b, c). No NH3 is formed under Ar gas (Fig. 4b), indicating the N element in the products mainly comes from the foreign N2 gas. The material also exhibits incremental ammonia yields over time, with a cumulative amount of approximately 330 μmol (2 h) with full spectrum light illumination under N2 gas. Similarly, there is no obvious increase of ammonia content in the solution when the light irradiation cut off. The photoreactions are driven by lighting.

Nitrogen temperature programmed desorption curves (N2-TPD) are further detected to evaluate the adsorption activities around the photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 4d, the peaks at (100−150) °C are assigned as the N2 physisorption process. The Ti-e sample shows one small absorption peak at approximately 342 °C, and the TiC-e sample displays two chemisorption peaks around 281 °C and 420 °C. The co-decorated sample (TiCoC-e) has not only the strongest chemisorption activity, but also the most active sites around 188 °C, 327 °C and 418 °C, respectively. Some reports have pointed that the N2 molecules chemisorption process plays a dominant role for the N2 photoreduction [45,46]. The modified sample of TiCoC-e has a highest N2 adsorption capacity and most chemisorption active sites.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (Uv-vis DRS) of the various samples are illustrated in Fig. 5a. The Ti-e, TiC-e and TiCo-e samples all present the light absorption edges at around 390 nm, which is close to the ultraviolet (UV) region. For the co-loaded sample (TiCoC-e), the intrinsic light absorption edge is approximately 430 nm. As shown in Fig. 5b, the bandgap is 3.19 eV, 3.16 eV, 3.12 eV and 2.91 eV for the Ti-e, TiCo-e, TiC-e and TiCoC-e, respectively. Moreover, the valence band (EVB) value are also obtained according to the VB-XPS curves of Ti-e, TiC-e, TiCo-e and TiCoC-e as 3.15 eV, 3.12 eV, 2.71 eV and 2.76 eV [47,48], as shown in Fig. 5c. The ECB positions can be calculated via the EVB and bandgaps, and the band structures (vs. NHE) are shown in Fig. 5d [49]. The self-decorated cobalt species are able to adjust the sample band structures. However, the absorbance curves of the co-decorated samples show a shoulder profile, demonstrating a stronger photo response in the visible light region (Fig.S6). The new sub-occupied energy level with approximately 1.61 eV of band gap according to the visible responsive region is estimated for the TiCoC-e sample (Fig. 5b), which are most likely to the substitution induced defect states. The similar result has also been reported in previous report [50].

The ammonia synthesis rates of the modified samples with different structural characteristics (TiCoC-e, TiCoC-m, and TiCoC-b) are also evaluated in Fig. 6a. Clearly, significant differences in photocatalytic performances are found among the catalysts with different exposed facets. The modified (001) exposed TiO2 sample (TiCoC-e) exhibits the best photocatalytic activity, followed by the commercial nano-TiO2 (TiCoC-b). The modified mesoscopic TiO2 (TiCoC-m) does not even have any photocatalytic N2 fixation activity. The calculated AQEs the catalysts are displayed in Fig. 6(b, c), and the TiCoC-e sample also has a higher data of nearly 0.64 % at 420 nm than that 0.21 % for TiCoC-b, indicating the higher utilization efficiency of optical energy for the exposed (001) facet sample. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy is used to estimate the chare charrier separation process in the photocatalysis. As shown in Fig. 6c, all the samples have the emission wavelength at about 450 nm assigned to the photogenerated carrier recombination process. Clearly, the TiCoC-e and TiCoC-b samples present the weak PL peaks, indicating the recombination efficiencies of photoinduced electron/hole pairs are low. TRPL plots show that the decay lifetime of charge carrier in TiCoC-e has been efficiently improved compared to the other two samples, and this sample also has the highest photocurrent density (Fig. 6d).

Energy band structures and improved N2 photo-fixation process on the modified samples can be described as the Fig. 6e. The co-decorated sample with the exposed (001) facet has the enhanced N2 adsorption capacity as well as the improved carriers separation efficiency, and the remarked NH3 generation rates can be reached predictably. The double dots of CDs/Co on the sample surface can act as the active sites to absorb and activate the N2 molecules. The photo-generated electrons are injected into the conduction band, and then migrate to the defects level on the exposed (001) facet where the N2 is reduced into NH3. These results indicate that the appropriate exposed crystal faces and suitable modification structures in the anatase TiO2 nano-materials can improve the N2 absorption and charge separation process to further promote the photocatalytic abilities.

In this study, composited dots co-decorated anatase titanium dioxide nanosheets on the exposed (001) crystal faces are assembled firstly using a common strategy. The co-decorated catalysts displayed an excellent photo response in the visible light region and a strongest chemisorption activity. The ammonia produced rates can achieve 162 or 83 μmol·gcal −1·h−1 under full-spectrum or visible light illumination without any additives, respectively, which increase up to 4 times compared to the original TiO2. Compared to the commercial nano-TiO2 (TiCoC-b) and mesoscopic TiO2 (TiCoC-m), the modified (001) exposed TiO2 nanosheets (TiCoC-e) also exhibits the best photocatalytic performance and apparent quantum efficiency. The appropriate exposed crystal faces and suitable modification structures in the anatase TiO2 nano-materials can improve the N2 absorption ability and photoinduced electron/hole pairs separation efficiency to further promote the photocatalytic activities. This work offers a promising design strategy of the photocatalysts.
Ge Song: Methodology. Huaiwei Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Qingwei Zhou: Investigation. Liang Bao: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The characterization results were supported by Beijing Zhongkebaice Technology Service Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2024.114034.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Challenges and opportunities for the photo-(thermal) synthesis of ammonia

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Mateo, A. Sousa, M. Zakharzhevskii, J. Gascon, Challenges and opportunities for the photo-(thermal) synthesis of ammonia, Green Chemistry, 26(2023) 1041-1061.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A comprehensive review on electrochemical green ammonia synthesis: from conventional to distinctive strategies for efficient nitrogen fixation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.R. Santhosh, R. Sankannavar, A comprehensive review on electrochemical green ammonia synthesis: From conventional to distinctive strategies for efficient nitrogen fixation, Applied Energy, 352 (2023).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in electrochemical synthesis of ammonia from nitrogen: strategies to improve the catalytic activity and selectivity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Yang, W. Ding, H. Zhang, S. Zhang, Recent progress in electrochemical synthesis of ammonia from nitrogen: strategies to improve the catalytic activity and selectivity, Energy & Environmental Science, 14 (2021) 672-687.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-based nanostructures for emerging photocatalysis: CO2 reduction, N2 fixation, and organic conversion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.Y. Xu, S. Li, M. Chen, J.M. Zhang, F. Rosei, Carbon-based nanostructures for emerging photocatalysis: CO2 reduction, N2 fixation, and organic conversion, Trends in Chemistry, 4 (2022) 984-1004.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Managing the nitrogen cycle via plasmonic (photo)electrocatalysis: toward circular economy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Nazemi, M.A. El-Sayed, Managing the Nitrogen Cycle via Plasmonic (Photo)Electrocatalysis: Toward Circular Economy, Accounts of Chemical Research, 54 (2021) 4294-4304.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress of indium-based photocatalysts: classification, regulation and diversified applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Band-structure tunability via the modulation of excitons in semiconductor nanostructures: manifestation in photocatalytic fuel generation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Ghosh, D. Sarkar, S. Bastia, Y.S. Chaudhary, Band-structure tunability via the modulation of excitons in semiconductor nanostructures: manifestation in photocatalytic fuel generation, Nanoscale, 15 (2023) 10939-10974.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ammonia as a carbon-free energy carrier: NH3 cracking to H-2

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Spatolisano, L.A. Pellegrini, A.R. de Angelis, S. Cattaneo, E. Roccaro, Ammonia as a Carbon-Free Energy Carrier: NH3 Cracking to H-2, Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research, 62 (2023) 10813-10827.
Y.H. Shi, Z.F. Zhao, D. Yang, J.D. Tan, X. Xin, Y.Q. Liu, Z.Y. Jiang, Engineering photocatalytic ammonia synthesis, Chemical Society Reviews, 52 (2023) 6938-6956.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress on electrocatalyst and photocatalyst design for nitrogen reduction
